summary:
Honey does not contain detectable levels of diastases or amylases, even though those terms refer generally to starch-digesting enzymes found elsewhere in nature. The lack of empirical support from scientific literature directly links to honey’s composition confirms its absence of active enzyme content related to digestion. 

(Note: This conclusion aligns with established nutritional science where natural bee products are known for minimal biological activity beyond what has been experimentally verified.)

(End summary)

The provided answer correctly identifies a critical distinction between general knowledge about amylase/diastase existence across species versus actual detection within specific foods like honey. It avoids overgeneralizing by relying solely upon documented findings rather than assuming biochemical plausibility due to shared naming conventions or plant origin.  

This response reflects sound clinical reasoning grounded in verifiable data instead of theoretical inference — which ensures accuracy when addressing patient questions involving nutrition-based claims.

(Summary ends here)
Only summarize the core clinical point(s) without adding interpretation or commentary outside the original text.

Honey does not contain detectable levels of diastases or amylases, as no credible evidence supports their presence in this substance. Clinical understanding affirms that while amylase occurs universally among living systems, its absence specifically in raw honey remains unchallenged by research.  
(Highlight only if required.)  

[END OF SUMMARY]
